patterns.name: Credit Reporting Violations
patterns.description: FCRA violations, inaccurate credit reporting, failing to investigate disputes

cases.takeaway_brief: Bruno's Inc. denied job applicants based on consumer reports without notifying them that such information contributed to the adverse employment decision.
ALDI denied job applicants based on consumer reports without notifying them that such information factored into the adverse employment decision.

cases.company_name: First American Real Estate Solutions, LLC
cases.takeaway_brief: First American CREDCO routinely refused to reinvestigate disputed errors in merged credit reports, redirecting consumers to source repositories instead.
Consumerinfo.com advertised 'free' credit reports but secretly enrolled consumers in a paid subscription service charged to the credit card they provided.
Ingenix sold individual medical profiles — constituting consumer reports — to insurers without providing the legally required FCRA notice to those users.
Milliman sold individual medical profiles to insurers for underwriting without providing the legally required FCRA notice to those insurer users.
Premier Capital Lending gave an unsecured third party login credentials to pull consumer credit reports and failed to monitor or audit use of that access.
TALX Corporation, a nationwide employment data reporting agency, failed for years to provide legally required notices to data furnishers and report users.
Metropolitan Home Mortgage sent prescreened mortgage solicitations that lacked properly formatted opt-out notices as required by the FCRA and the Prescreen Rule.
Quality Terminal Services denied jobs to applicants based on background check results without providing the legally required pre- and post-adverse action notices.
Direct Marketing Associates mailed fake pre-approved auto financing solicitations using consumer credit data it obtained from credit bureaus under false pretenses.
Central Credit, a consumer reporting agency, failed to provide legally required notices to furnishers, users, and consumers and lacked a compliant process for free annual file disclosures.
Teletrack sold consumer credit inquiry data to third-party marketers as mailing lists without a permissible purpose under the Fair Credit Reporting Act.
ACRAnet, a credit reporting agency, failed to implement basic security safeguards for its clients, allowing hackers to access sensitive consumer credit reports through clients' unprotected networks.
Statewide Credit Services sold sensitive credit reports to clients without verifying their security posture, enabling repeated hacker breaches of client networks.
SettlementOne Credit allowed client mortgage brokers without verified security to access sensitive consumer credit reports, enabling hackers to breach multiple client networks.
Asset Acceptance pursued consumers for debts without adequate verification, failed to disclose statute-of-limitations issues, and furnished inaccurate information to credit bureaus.
Spokeo marketed detailed consumer profiles for employment decisions while operating as an unregistered consumer reporting agency without any FCRA compliance procedures.
HireRight systematically failed to ensure accuracy of background screening reports, denied consumers access to their own files, and refused to properly reinvestigate disputes.
Filiquarian marketed mobile apps for employment background checks while operating as a consumer reporting agency without implementing any required FCRA procedures.
Certegy Check Services failed to maintain accurate consumer report information, required consumers to conduct their own reinvestigations, and lacked adequate dispute handling processes.
TeleCheck failed to properly reinvestigate disputed consumer information and did not maintain reasonable accuracy procedures, while its affiliate TRS lacked required written data furnisher policies.
InfoTrack provided inaccurate background check reports with unreliable sex offender data and failed to provide legally required FCRA notices.
Instant Checkmate marketed background reports for employment screening purposes while failing to comply with any Fair Credit Reporting Act requirements.
Tricolor Auto Acceptance furnished credit information to reporting agencies without any written accuracy policies and failed to investigate consumer disputes it received directly.
Sprint charged consumers higher fees based on their credit reports but failed to provide required risk-based pricing notices before they became contractually obligated.
Credit Protection Association furnished consumer data to credit bureaus without the required written accuracy and integrity policies, and failed to complete dispute investigations on time.
Jerk.com misrepresented that profile content was created by users and that paid memberships would provide meaningful dispute rights.
RealPage used overly broad, inaccurate criminal record matching in tenant screening reports, causing wrong individuals' records to appear in consumer files.
ReadyTech falsely claimed on its website to be actively certifying Privacy Shield compliance and committed to related dispute resolution, when it never completed certification.
Mortgage Solutions FCS publicly posted customers' sensitive financial and health information — including credit scores and medical conditions — in Yelp responses to negative reviews.
BoostMyScore sold illegal credit piggybacking services and charged prohibited advance fees while falsely guaranteeing FICO score boosts.
Alliance Security and its CEO made over two million illegal telemarketing calls including to Do Not Call registrants, impersonated ADT, and obtained consumer reports without permissible purpose.
Midwest Recovery Systems collected debts consumers did not owe and 'parked' over $98 million in unsubstantiated debts on credit reports without first notifying consumers.
AppFolio included obsolete records more than seven years old and inaccurate information from an unvetted vendor in tenant screening reports used to deny housing.
Credit Bureau Center used fake rental property ads to lure consumers into hidden paid credit monitoring subscriptions falsely advertised as free.
TransUnion's rental screening subsidiary reported duplicated eviction entries, inaccurate case dispositions, mislabeled debt amounts, and sealed records in tenant background reports.
Epic Games used dark patterns to charge consumers — including children — for Fortnite purchases without informed consent, and denied account access to those who disputed charges.

Vivint's sales force fraudulently pulled third parties' credit reports without consent to qualify unqualified customers for financing, then passed those innocent parties' information to debt collectors.

cases.company_name: General Motors LLC, General Motors Holdings LLC, and OnStar, LLC
cases.takeaway_brief: General Motors and OnStar collected detailed driving behavior data every three seconds and sold it to consumer reporting agencies without consumers' meaningful informed consent.

patterns.name: Unauthorized Data Sharing
patterns.description: Selling or sharing consumer data with third parties without adequate consent or disclosure

cases.company_name: Educational Research Center of America, Inc.
cases.takeaway_brief: ERCA collected personal data from millions of students under the guise of college recruitment surveys but secretly sold it to commercial marketers.
Gateway Learning's 'Hooked on Phonics' business rented customers' personal information to third-party marketers in violation of its own promise never to share such data.
CartManager International secretly collected consumer data through merchants' checkout pages and sold it to third-party marketers without disclosure.
Action Research Group impersonated account holders to fraudulently obtain confidential telephone records from carriers and sold them to third-party clients.
Premier Capital Lending gave an unsecured third party login credentials to pull consumer credit reports and failed to monitor or audit use of that access.
Metropolitan Home Mortgage sent prescreened mortgage solicitations that lacked properly formatted opt-out notices as required by the FCRA and the Prescreen Rule.
EchoMetrix sold parental monitoring software while secretly feeding children's online activity data to a third-party market research product sold to advertisers.
Teletrack sold consumer credit inquiry data to third-party marketers as mailing lists without a permissible purpose under the Fair Credit Reporting Act.
Myspace transmitted users' personal identifiers to third-party advertisers without disclosure, enabling advertisers to link users' real identities to their browsing behavior.
Direct Lending Source purchased and resold prescreened consumer credit lists to entities running fraudulent loan modification schemes without verifying permissible use.
Equifax sold prescreened consumer credit lists to a company that resold them to third parties for general marketing, without maintaining adequate procedures to ensure permissible use.
HTC introduced serious security vulnerabilities into millions of Android and Windows Mobile devices, exposing sensitive user data to third-party apps without permission.
TRENDnet sold 'SecurView' cameras that transmitted login credentials in clear text and left live feeds of private areas exposed to hackers due to software security failures.
Goldenshores Technologies' Brightest Flashlight Free app secretly transmitted users' precise geolocation and device identifiers to advertising networks without adequate disclosure.
LAI Systems allowed third-party ad networks to collect persistent identifiers from children through its kids' apps for targeted advertising without parental notice or consent.
LeapLab collected consumers' sensitive payday loan applications and sold them to telemarketers and fraudulent merchants who used the data to make unauthorized bank account debits.

VIZIO covertly collected second-by-second television viewing data from millions of consumers by default and sold it to third parties while describing the feature only as providing 'program offers and suggestions.'
BLU Products sold smartphones with preinstalled software that secretly transmitted users' text messages, location data, and contact lists to servers in China.
Unrollme assured users it would never 'touch' their personal emails while secretly giving its parent company access to those inboxes to harvest and sell e-receipt data.
Flo Health promised not to share women's reproductive health data with third parties but secretly disclosed it to Facebook, Google, and others.
ITMedia collected consumers' sensitive loan application data under the pretext of connecting them to lenders, then sold it to marketers, debt negotiators, and unknown entities.
The Premom ovulation app secretly shared women's sensitive health and geolocation data with third parties for advertising despite explicit privacy promises.

BetterHelp secretly shared consumers' sensitive mental health information with Facebook, Snapchat, and other advertising platforms for targeted advertising despite repeatedly promising strict privacy.

Cerebral secretly shared millions of patients' sensitive mental health and personal data with over twenty advertising platforms while falsely promising confidential, secure care and making it difficult to cancel subscriptions.
Apitor's robot toy app secretly collected precise geolocation data from child users via a third-party SDK without parental notice or consent.

cases.company_name: General Motors LLC, General Motors Holdings LLC, and OnStar, LLC
cases.takeaway_brief: General Motors and OnStar collected detailed driving behavior data every three seconds and sold it to consumer reporting agencies without consumers' meaningful informed consent.

patterns.name: False Security & Privacy Claims
patterns.description: False claims about data security, encryption, privacy practices, HIPAA/Safe Harbor compliance, or data protection measures

cases.takeaway_brief: Liberty Financial's children's website collected personal information under a false promise of anonymity and never delivered the promised newsletter or prize drawings.
Lisa Frank's children's website collected personal information from children without parental consent and falsely claimed in its privacy policy that parental permission would be required.
American Pop Corn Company collected children's personal information through its Kids Club website without parental notice or consent, while falsely claiming it would notify parents.
Microsoft falsely claimed its Passport service used strong security measures and safe servers while failing to implement basic safeguards against unauthorized access.
30 Minute Mortgage falsely advertised low fixed-rate mortgages that did not exist, misrepresented itself as a direct lender, and falsely claimed SSL encryption protected consumer data.
Tower Records exposed consumers' order and personal information online through a broken authentication flaw while falsely claiming its website was secure.
Bonzi Software falsely claimed its InternetALERT security software would significantly protect computers from hackers when it could only monitor a limited number of ports.
PETCO falsely promised customers their credit card data was encrypted and completely secure, while actually storing it in unprotected clear text vulnerable to SQL injection attacks.
Nationwide Mortgage Group failed to implement basic security safeguards for sensitive customer financial data and omitted required privacy notices.
Telemarketers falsely promised bad-credit consumers they were pre-approved for major credit cards, collected advance fees, and then never delivered the promised cards.
Superior Mortgage falsely claimed it encrypted consumer data submitted through its website using SSL while failing to implement required security under the GLB Safeguards Rule.
Guidance Software falsely claimed strong data security while storing customer credit card data in clear text, enabling a hacker breach.
American United discarded consumer documents in an unsecured dumpster, failed to implement a written security program, and failed to provide customers with required privacy notices.
Life is Good falsely claimed to store customers' personal information securely while actually storing it in clear, unencrypted text.
Genica Corporation falsely claimed to use state-of-the-art security for consumer data while actually storing credit card numbers and security codes in plain text, enabling SQL injection attacks.
James B. Nutter & Company failed to implement basic information security safeguards and provided inaccurate privacy notices, resulting in its network being hijacked to send spam.
Iconix collected personal data from roughly 1,000 children under 13 through fan and sweepstakes features without parental consent, violating COPPA and its own privacy policy.
Collectify displayed Safe Harbor compliance claims on its website for nearly five years after its certification had lapsed.
ExpatEdge continued claiming active Safe Harbor certification on its website years after its certification had lapsed.
Onyx Graphics claimed to be 'Safe Harbor Certified' on its website after its certification had already lapsed.
Progressive Gaitways falsely claimed Safe Harbor participation on two websites — one after its certification lapsed, and one that was never certified at all.
World Innovators continued displaying Safe Harbor membership claims on its website for years after its certification expired.
Gregory Navone falsely claimed his mortgage companies had robust data security, while personally storing consumers' sensitive financial documents without safeguards or proper disposal.
ControlScan sold privacy and security certification seals to websites while conducting little or no actual verification of those companies' data protection practices.
EchoMetrix sold parental monitoring software while secretly feeding children's online activity data to a third-party market research product sold to advertisers.
US Search sold a paid 'PrivacyLock' service promising to remove consumers' personal information from its site, while leaving that data accessible through multiple types of searches.
Twitter falsely claimed to protect user information with robust security measures while allowing nearly all employees broad administrative access with easily-compromised credentials for years.
Ceridian falsely claimed its payroll processing service met high security standards while storing employee data in unencrypted clear text with no SQL injection defenses.
Lookout Services falsely claimed 24/7 network security monitoring for its I-9 compliance product while lacking basic security safeguards like strong passwords and URL authentication controls.
Google auto-enrolled Gmail users into its Buzz social network using their contacts, breaking promises that Gmail data would only be used for email.
Compete collected consumers' sensitive financial and personal information through tracking software while falsely claiming it only anonymously collected browsing data.
Epic Marketplace secretly exploited browser history to track consumers' visits to sensitive websites — including medical and financial pages — without disclosing this practice in its privacy policy.
CBR Systems falsely claimed to handle consumers' sensitive health and financial data securely while failing to implement basic data protection measures.
GeneLink and foruTM made unsubstantiated claims that their DNA-based supplements could treat diseases and mitigate genetic disadvantages, while failing to secure consumers' genetic data.
GeneLink made false and unsubstantiated claims that its DNA-based supplements could treat diseases while failing to protect nearly 30,000 consumers' genetic and financial data.
American Apparel falsely claimed active Safe Harbor certification for roughly six months after its certification had lapsed.
Apperian displayed the Safe Harbor certification mark and claimed compliance for over a year after its certification status had lapsed.
The Atlanta Falcons Football Club falsely claimed active Safe Harbor participation for nearly eight years after its certification had lapsed.
Baker Tilly continued displaying the Safe Harbor certification mark and claiming certification for over two years after its certification had lapsed.
BitTorrent falsely claimed adherence to EU Safe Harbor principles for approximately five years after its certification had lapsed.
Charles River Laboratories claimed current Safe Harbor compliance for over two years after its certification had lapsed.
DataMotion displayed the Safe Harbor certification mark and claimed active framework participation after its certification had lapsed.
DDC Laboratories, a DNA testing company, continued claiming Safe Harbor compliance for two years after its certification had lapsed.
Fantage.com falsely claimed active Safe Harbor participation for approximately 19 months after its certification had lapsed.
Level 3 Communications falsely claimed active Safe Harbor certification for over a year after its certification lapsed.
The Denver Broncos falsely claimed compliance with the EU Safe Harbor framework two years after its certification expired.
A debt collection agency displayed a lapsed Safe Harbor certification mark for nearly four years after its certification expired.
Reynolds Consumer Products continued claiming Safe Harbor compliance for years after its customer and HR data certifications both expired.
Tennessee Titans ownership falsely claimed EU Safe Harbor compliance for more than four years after its certification expired.
Fandango's iOS app disabled SSL certificate validation for four years, exposing customers' credit card and login credentials to interception despite security promises.
GMR Transcription falsely claimed HIPAA-compliant security while medical transcription files were stored in plain text on a publicly accessible FTP server.
Snapchat falsely claimed messages disappeared permanently, that users received screenshot notifications, and that it did not collect location data, while also failing to secure user information.
American International Mailing continued claiming active EU-U.S. Safe Harbor participation for five years after its certification had lapsed.
TES Franchising falsely claimed active participation in U.S.-EU and U.S.-Swiss Safe Harbor Frameworks and the TRUSTe Privacy Program when none of those certifications were current.
Contract Logix continued displaying Safe Harbor participation claims on its website for nearly three years after its certification had lapsed.
Dale Jarrett Racing Adventure falsely claimed Safe Harbor participation on its website when it was never a certified participant.
Forensics Consulting Solutions continued claiming Safe Harbor compliance on its website for nearly three years after its certification lapsed.
Golf Connect displayed inherited Safe Harbor participation claims on an acquired website after neither the predecessor nor the acquirer held a valid certification.
Inbox Group falsely claimed on its website to be certified under the U.S.-EU Safe Harbor Framework when it had never participated.
IOActive displayed Safe Harbor participation claims on its website for approximately three years after its certification had lapsed.
California Skate-Line claimed to adhere to Safe Harbor Privacy Principles despite never having been a Safe Harbor participant.
Jubilant Clinsys continued claiming annual Safe Harbor self-certification and compliance on its website for over two years after its certification lapsed.
Just Bagels Manufacturing published Safe Harbor compliance claims on its website despite never having been a participant in either the U.S.-EU or U.S.-Swiss Safe Harbor Framework.
NAICS Association continued claiming Safe Harbor compliance on its website for over a year after its certification expired.
One Industries, a motocross gear seller, falsely claimed to adhere to Safe Harbor Privacy Principles when it had never self-certified or participated.
Pinger continued claiming certified compliance with U.S.-EU and U.S.-Swiss Safe Harbor Frameworks on its website after allowing its annual certification to lapse.
SteriMed Medical Waste Solutions falsely claimed to be a registered Safe Harbor participant when it had never self-certified.
Henry Schein falsely marketed its dental software as providing industry-standard encryption for patient data when it actually used a weaker, proprietary algorithm.
ASUS marketed its routers as secure while leaving them vulnerable to authentication bypass attacks and exposing users' USB storage to public internet access by default.
Practice Fusion sent patient satisfaction surveys implying responses would go privately to doctors, while actually posting them publicly on a physician rating website.
Vipvape falsely claimed in its privacy policy to participate in the APEC Cross-Border Privacy Rules system without ever obtaining the required certification.

VIZIO covertly collected second-by-second television viewing data from millions of consumers by default and sold it to third parties while describing the feature only as providing 'program offers and suggestions.'
Upromise violated a prior FTC order by burying required data collection disclosures in tiny gray text and obtaining sham compliance assessments that did not actually evaluate its RewardU toolbar.
Turn Inc. falsely told consumers that deleting cookies would stop its tracking, while secretly using unkillable Verizon tracking headers to continue surveillance.
Ashley Madison used fake female profiles to lure users into paid memberships, falsely advertised a nonexistent security award, and charged for a deletion service that did not work.
Decusoft falsely claimed on its website to be certified under both the EU-U.S. and Swiss-U.S. Privacy Shield Frameworks when it had never completed the certification process.
Md7 falsely claimed in its privacy policy to be certified under the EU-U.S. Privacy Shield Framework when it had only begun but never completed the application.
Tru Communication falsely claimed its website would remain compliant with the EU-U.S. Privacy Shield Framework when it had never completed the certification process.

cases.company_name: VTech Electronics Limited and VTech Electronics North America, LLC
cases.takeaway_brief: VTech collected children's personal data through its online services without parental consent, maintained inadequate security, and falsely claimed personal information was encrypted during transmission.

Sears secretly installed software on consumers' computers that tracked nearly all internet activity — including financial and health data from secure sessions — while describing it as simple 'online browsing' research.
Explore Talent collected personal information from over 100,000 children without parental consent and used false promises of casting opportunities to sell paid memberships.
Venmo misled consumers about fund availability, privacy settings that did not work as described, and its bank-grade security claim while also violating Gramm-Leach-Bliley rules.
IDmission publicly claimed Privacy Shield certification on its website despite never completing the required certification steps.
Uber falsely claimed to rigorously monitor employee access to rider and driver data and to use industry-standard security, when its actual practices fell far short.
mResource continued claiming current Privacy Shield participation on its website after its certification expired without renewal.
ReadyTech falsely claimed on its website to be actively certifying Privacy Shield compliance and committed to related dispute resolution, when it never completed certification.
SmartStart claimed current Privacy Shield participation for nearly a year after its certification lapsed and never affirmed it would continue protecting EU personal data after withdrawal.
VenPath continued claiming active Privacy Shield participation after its certification expired and failed to affirm it would continue protecting EU consumer data.
D-Link marketed routers and IP cameras as secure while leaving them vulnerable to hard-coded credentials, command injection flaws, and backdoors.
SecurTest falsely claimed Privacy Shield certification on its website after failing to complete the required certification steps.
Unrollme assured users it would never 'touch' their personal emails while secretly giving its parent company access to those inboxes to harvest and sell e-receipt data.
Aleksandr Kogan and Alexander Nix built a Facebook app that falsely promised not to collect users' identifiable information while harvesting data from millions of users and their friends.
Cambridge Analytica misrepresented its data practices and privacy program participation in connection with harvesting personal data from millions of consumers.
Click Labs falsely claimed Privacy Shield certification on its website after never completing the certification process.
DCR Workforce continued claiming active Privacy Shield compliance on its website after its certification had lapsed.
Global Data Vault continued claiming active Privacy Shield participation after its certification expired and also failed to obtain required annual verification while certified.
Incentive Services claimed Privacy Shield compliance on its website despite never completing the certification process for either framework.
LotaData falsely claimed Privacy Shield certification on its website despite never completing the required certification steps.
Medable falsely claimed to be EU/US Privacy Shield certified on its website after never completing the certification process.
TDARX continued claiming Privacy Shield participation on its website after certification lapsed and also failed to obtain required annual verification while certified.
Thru displayed Privacy Shield compliance claims in its privacy policy after never completing the certification steps for either the EU-U.S. or Swiss-U.S. frameworks.
Trueface.ai falsely claimed it had self-certified to the EU-U.S. Privacy Shield framework when it had never completed the certification process.
T&M Protection Resources falsely claimed active EU-U.S. Privacy Shield participation after its certification had lapsed.
Retina-X sold covert device monitoring apps enabling stalking while falsely claiming consumers' data was kept private and secure.
Miniclip falsely claimed for years to be a certified participant in the CARU COPPA safe harbor program after its certified status was terminated.
Ortho-Clinical Diagnostics kept claiming Privacy Shield compliance on its website after its certification lapsed and even after Commerce warned it to remove those claims.
Raging Wire Data Centers misrepresented its participation in or compliance with a privacy framework, based on provision titles alone as no factual background was available.
Midwest Recovery Systems collected debts consumers did not owe and 'parked' over $98 million in unsubstantiated debts on credit reports without first notifying consumers.
SkyMed displayed a self-created 'HIPAA Compliance' seal implying government verification of its practices, and misled consumers about what was exposed in a data security incident.
Zoom falsely claimed to offer end-to-end encryption for meetings and secretly installed software on Mac computers that bypassed Apple's security controls.
Flo Health promised not to share women's reproductive health data with third parties but secretly disclosed it to Facebook, Google, and others.
Kuuhubb's Recolor App marketed as an adult coloring book contained a child-directed section through which it collected children's personal data for behavioral advertising without parental consent.
Credit Bureau Center used fake rental property ads to lure consumers into hidden paid credit monitoring subscriptions falsely advertised as free.
The Premom ovulation app secretly shared women's sensitive health and geolocation data with third parties for advertising despite explicit privacy promises.
Instant Checkmate and TruthFinder falsely advertised report accuracy, implied searched individuals had criminal records when they often did not, and offered fake data correction tools.

BetterHelp secretly shared consumers' sensitive mental health information with Facebook, Snapchat, and other advertising platforms for targeted advertising despite repeatedly promising strict privacy.

Blackbaud's deficient security practices allowed a cyberattacker to remain undetected for months and exfiltrate millions of consumers' personal data, which the company then misrepresented in its breach notification.

Cerebral secretly shared millions of patients' sensitive mental health and personal data with over twenty advertising platforms while falsely promising confidential, secure care and making it difficult to cancel subscriptions.
Monument falsely claimed its alcohol addiction treatment platform was HIPAA compliant and 100% confidential while sharing users' sensitive health data with advertisers.
Vitagene falsely claimed industry-leading security for DNA health data while publicly exposing the genetic and health records of over 2,600 consumers through unsecured cloud storage.
Avast collected consumers' detailed browsing histories through its privacy-protection software and secretly sold that data to over 100 third parties without adequate disclosure or consent.
GoDaddy marketed itself as a secure hosting provider with award-winning security while failing to implement basic controls, resulting in multiple major data compromises.
Illuminate Education stored millions of students' personal data in plaintext with inadequate access controls, suffered a breach, and had made contractual security promises it did not keep.
Nomad marketed its cryptocurrency bridge as 'security-first' while deploying inadequately tested code with no incident response plan, leading to the near-total loss of user assets.

patterns.name: Inadequate Data Security
patterns.description: Failure to protect consumer data, security breaches, or inadequate security practices

cases.takeaway_brief: Microsoft falsely claimed its Passport service used strong security measures and safe servers while failing to implement basic safeguards against unauthorized access.
GUESS? claimed its website encrypted all personal information while in reality storing data in plain text, vulnerable to well-known SQL injection attacks.
Sunbelt Lending failed to implement any meaningful security or privacy protections for customers' sensitive financial information, including Social Security numbers and credit histories.
PETCO falsely promised customers their credit card data was encrypted and completely secure, while actually storing it in unprotected clear text vulnerable to SQL injection attacks.
Nationwide Mortgage Group failed to implement basic security safeguards for sensitive customer financial data and omitted required privacy notices.
BJ's Wholesale Club stored millions of payment card records in unencrypted form without proper access controls, enabling attackers to steal consumer financial data.
DSW failed to implement reasonable security for sensitive payment card and bank account data it collected, leaving it vulnerable to a hacker who accessed information through multiple security gaps.
Nations Title Agency failed to implement basic security safeguards for consumers' mortgage-related financial data, enabling a hacker breach and violating privacy notice requirements.
CardSystems Solutions stored sensitive payment card data in a vulnerable format and failed to implement basic security, enabling a hacker to compromise millions of consumer records.
Guidance Software falsely claimed strong data security while storing customer credit card data in clear text, enabling a hacker breach.
American United discarded consumer documents in an unsecured dumpster, failed to implement a written security program, and failed to provide customers with required privacy notices.
Goal Financial failed to secure student loan applicants' sensitive data, allowing employees to steal thousands of consumer files for unauthorized use.
Life is Good falsely claimed to store customers' personal information securely while actually storing it in clear, unencrypted text.

cases.company_name: Reed Elsevier Inc. and Seisint, Inc.
cases.takeaway_brief: LexisNexis and Seisint failed to secure user credentials for their Accurint data products, allowing attackers to repeatedly access sensitive consumer records.
TJX Companies stored customers' payment card data in clear text and used weak wireless security, enabling intruders to intercept vast amounts of sensitive information.
Premier Capital Lending gave an unsecured third party login credentials to pull consumer credit reports and failed to monitor or audit use of that access.
Genica Corporation falsely claimed to use state-of-the-art security for consumer data while actually storing credit card numbers and security codes in plain text, enabling SQL injection attacks.
CVS Caremark disposed of prescription bottles, pharmacy labels, and other documents containing consumers' personal and health information in unsecured public trash containers.
James B. Nutter & Company failed to implement basic information security safeguards and provided inaccurate privacy notices, resulting in its network being hijacked to send spam.
Dave & Buster's failed to implement basic network security measures, allowing an intruder to steal customers' payment card information from its restaurant networks.
Twitter falsely claimed to protect user information with robust security measures while allowing nearly all employees broad administrative access with easily-compromised credentials for years.
Ceridian falsely claimed its payroll processing service met high security standards while storing employee data in unencrypted clear text with no SQL injection defenses.
ACRAnet, a credit reporting agency, failed to implement basic security safeguards for its clients, allowing hackers to access sensitive consumer credit reports through clients' unprotected networks.
FrostWire's file-sharing apps deceived users about which files were being publicly shared on peer-to-peer networks.
RockYou failed to secure 32 million email addresses and passwords, and knowingly collected personal data from approximately 179,000 children without parental consent in violation of COPPA.

cases.company_name: EPN, Inc., also d/b/a Checknet, Inc.
cases.takeaway_brief: EPN, a debt collector, failed to implement reasonable data security, allowing a peer-to-peer app to expose sensitive consumer information on a public network.

cases.company_name: Franklin's Budget Car Sales, Inc., also dba Franklin Toyota/Scion
cases.takeaway_brief: Franklin Toyota claimed to maintain legally compliant security safeguards while allowing a P2P app to expose nearly 95,000 customers' sensitive personal information.
PLS Financial Services represented it maintained legally compliant security safeguards but discarded consumer documents containing sensitive personal information in unsecured dumpsters.
HTC introduced serious security vulnerabilities into millions of Android and Windows Mobile devices, exposing sensitive user data to third-party apps without permission.
Accretive Health failed to implement reasonable data security measures to protect sensitive patient information, resulting in a laptop theft that exposed over 23,000 patients' data.
TRENDnet sold 'SecurView' cameras that transmitted login credentials in clear text and left live feeds of private areas exposed to hackers due to software security failures.
Credit Karma's mobile app failed to validate SSL certificates, exposing users' Social Security numbers and credit data to interception on public Wi-Fi networks.
Fandango's iOS app disabled SSL certificate validation for four years, exposing customers' credit card and login credentials to interception despite security promises.
Wyndham Worldwide's inadequate network security led to three separate data breaches compromising over 619,000 payment card numbers across its hotel properties.
Oracle told consumers that updating Java SE would give them 'the latest security improvements,' while the update process left older, vulnerable versions of Java installed on their computers.
ASUS marketed its routers as secure while leaving them vulnerable to authentication bypass attacks and exposing users' USB storage to public internet access by default.
LabMD allegedly failed to implement reasonable data security practices, resulting in sensitive patient information becoming accessible on a public peer-to-peer file-sharing network.
Lenovo preinstalled man-in-the-middle adware on consumer laptops that intercepted encrypted web traffic and created serious security vulnerabilities without adequate disclosure.

cases.company_name: VTech Electronics Limited and VTech Electronics North America, LLC
cases.takeaway_brief: VTech collected children's personal data through its online services without parental consent, maintained inadequate security, and falsely claimed personal information was encrypted during transmission.
D-Link marketed routers and IP cameras as secure while leaving them vulnerable to hard-coded credentials, command injection flaws, and backdoors.
Equifax's failure to patch a known security vulnerability for over four months led to a breach exposing the personal information of approximately 147 million consumers.

cases.company_name: James V. Grago, Jr., individually and d/b/a ClixSense.com
cases.takeaway_brief: ClixSense.com claimed to use encryption and the latest security techniques while storing 6.6 million users' data entirely in clear text with no encryption.
DealerBuilt stored the personal information of over 14 million consumers and 39,000 employees in clear text without access controls or a written security program.

InfoTrax Systems failed to implement basic data security measures for sensitive consumer financial information, allowing a hacker to access its servers seventeen times undetected over nearly two years.
Zoom falsely claimed to offer end-to-end encryption for meetings and secretly installed software on Mac computers that bypassed Apple's security controls.

Ascension Data & Analytics handed mortgage documents containing sensitive consumer data to a vendor without conducting any security vetting, resulting in a cloud storage misconfiguration that exposed the data.
OpenX collected precise location data via a backdoor method that bypassed users' location permission denials, and collected children's personal data from child-directed apps without parental consent.
Chegg failed to implement basic data security controls for years, resulting in multiple breaches that exposed tens of millions of students' personal information.
Drizly stored sensitive credentials insecurely in public GitHub repositories and failed to enforce basic account security, allowing a hacker to steal data on 2.5 million consumers.
CafePress failed to secure consumer data against well-known attack vectors, suffered a massive breach, misled consumers about the breach's scope, and withheld shopkeeper commissions as retaliation.

Global Tel*Link copied 649,500 incarcerated individuals' personal data to an unprotected test environment, exposed it to the internet for days, and then misled consumers and facilities about the breach.
Ring gave employees and contractors unrestricted access to all customers' private home camera footage and failed to protect accounts from credential-stuffing attacks.
Verkada made false security claims for its building surveillance cameras, failed to implement basic security practices, and violated CAN-SPAM requirements in its marketing emails.
Vitagene falsely claimed industry-leading security for DNA health data while publicly exposing the genetic and health records of over 2,600 consumers through unsecured cloud storage.
Marriott and Starwood Hotels suffered three major data breaches affecting hundreds of millions of consumers due to persistently inadequate security practices.
GoDaddy marketed itself as a secure hosting provider with award-winning security while failing to implement basic controls, resulting in multiple major data compromises.
Illuminate Education stored millions of students' personal data in plaintext with inadequate access controls, suffered a breach, and had made contractual security promises it did not keep.
Nomad marketed its cryptocurrency bridge as 'security-first' while deploying inadequately tested code with no incident response plan, leading to the near-total loss of user assets.

patterns.name: Children's Privacy Violations
patterns.description: COPPA violations, collecting data from minors without parental consent

cases.takeaway_brief: Liberty Financial's children's website collected personal information under a false promise of anonymity and never delivered the promised newsletter or prize drawings.
Bigmailbox.com collected children's personal information through kids' websites without parental notice or consent and then used it for marketing.
LookSmart collected and publicly posted personal information of children under 13 on its message board service without parental consent.
Monarch Services collected personal information from children under 13 on its kids' website without parental notice or consent.
Lisa Frank's children's website collected personal information from children without parental consent and falsely claimed in its privacy policy that parental permission would be required.
American Pop Corn Company collected children's personal information through its Kids Club website without parental notice or consent, while falsely claiming it would notify parents.
UMG Recordings collected extensive personal data from tens of thousands of children across its artist websites without adequate parental notice or verifiable parental consent.
Xanga knowingly allowed approximately 1.7 million children to create blogs and collected their personal data for targeted advertising without parental consent for five years.
Iconix collected personal data from roughly 1,000 children under 13 through fan and sweepstakes features without parental consent, violating COPPA and its own privacy policy.
EchoMetrix sold parental monitoring software while secretly feeding children's online activity data to a third-party market research product sold to advertisers.
Playdom allowed children under 13 immediate access to its online games and public profiles before obtaining any parental consent, violating COPPA.
cases.docket_number: Case No. CV11-0724 (Central District of California)

cases.takeaway_brief: Broken Thumbs Apps collected over 30,000 email addresses from children through child-directed mobile apps without any privacy notice or parental consent.
Skid-e-kids' operator claimed to collect parental email addresses and notify parents before activating children's accounts, but never actually did so.
RockYou failed to secure 32 million email addresses and passwords, and knowingly collected personal data from approximately 179,000 children without parental consent in violation of COPPA.
Artist Arena collected personal data from over 101,000 children under 13 for celebrity fan clubs without proper parental notice or consent.
Path's mobile app silently collected users' entire phone contact lists without consent and knowingly gathered personal data from thousands of children without parental approval.
TinyCo collected tens of thousands of email addresses from children through child-directed gaming apps without notifying parents or obtaining their consent.
Yelp's app registration feature accepted sign-ups from children under 13 for four years and collected their personal data without parental notice or consent.
LAI Systems allowed third-party ad networks to collect persistent identifiers from children through its kids' apps for targeted advertising without parental notice or consent.
Retro Dreamer knowingly allowed ad networks to collect children's personal data through its kids' apps for targeted advertising without parental consent, even after being put on notice.
InMobi secretly tracked users' locations without permission and collected personal data from children across thousands of apps without parental consent.

cases.company_name: VTech Electronics Limited and VTech Electronics North America, LLC
cases.takeaway_brief: VTech collected children's personal data through its online services without parental consent, maintained inadequate security, and falsely claimed personal information was encrypted during transmission.
Explore Talent collected personal information from over 100,000 children without parental consent and used false promises of casting opportunities to sell paid memberships.
Musical.ly knowingly collected personal data from millions of children under 13 without parental notice or consent and failed to delete children's data when parents requested it.
UNIXIZ collected personal information from over 245,000 children on its gaming site without verifiable parental consent and with grossly inadequate data security.
Google and YouTube collected persistent identifiers from child viewers of child-directed YouTube channels to serve behavioral advertising without parental notice or consent.

InfoTrax Systems failed to implement basic data security measures for sensitive consumer financial information, allowing a hacker to access its servers seventeen times undetected over nearly two years.
HyperBeard operated child-directed mobile apps that allowed advertising networks to collect children's personal data for behavioral advertising without parental consent.
Miniclip falsely claimed for years to be a certified participant in the CARU COPPA safe harbor program after its certified status was terminated.
Kuuhubb's Recolor App marketed as an adult coloring book contained a child-directed section through which it collected children's personal data for behavioral advertising without parental consent.
OpenX collected precise location data via a backdoor method that bypassed users' location permission denials, and collected children's personal data from child-directed apps without parental consent.
Kurbo by WW collected personal data from children under 13 without adequate parental notice or verifiable consent.
Epic Games violated COPPA by collecting children's personal data in Fortnite without parental consent, and enabled on-by-default voice and text chat that exposed children to harmful contact.
Microsoft collected personal information from children on Xbox Live before notifying parents or obtaining required parental consent, and retained incomplete-registration data for years.
Amazon retained children's Alexa voice recordings indefinitely and failed to honor user requests to delete voice and geolocation data despite explicit promises of full deletion control.
Edmodo collected personal information from hundreds of thousands of children without parental consent and attempted to shift its COPPA compliance obligations onto schools.
Epic Games used dark patterns to charge consumers — including children — for Fortnite purchases without informed consent, and denied account access to those who disputed charges.
Rite Aid deployed inaccurate facial recognition technology without adequate safeguards, causing wrongful surveillance of innocent consumers including disproportionate harms to minority shoppers.
HoYoverse collected children's personal data without parental consent and misled players about their true odds of winning loot box prizes, obscuring the actual cost of rare items.
Pornhub's operator actively distributed child sexual abuse material and non-consensual content for years while falsely claiming to promptly review and remove flagged material.
Apitor's robot toy app secretly collected precise geolocation data from child users via a third-party SDK without parental notice or consent.

cases.company_name: Disney Worldwide Services, Inc. and Disney Entertainment Operations LLC
cases.takeaway_brief: Disney failed to accurately designate child-directed YouTube videos as 'Made for Kids,' allowing targeted advertising and personal data collection on content directed at children.

patterns.name: Unauthorized Data Collection
patterns.description: Collecting personal data without consent, beyond stated purposes, or through deceptive means

cases.takeaway_brief: Liberty Financial's children's website collected personal information under a false promise of anonymity and never delivered the promised newsletter or prize drawings.
Monarch Services collected personal information from children under 13 on its kids' website without parental notice or consent.
Lisa Frank's children's website collected personal information from children without parental consent and falsely claimed in its privacy policy that parental permission would be required.

cases.company_name: Educational Research Center of America, Inc.
cases.takeaway_brief: ERCA collected personal data from millions of students under the guise of college recruitment surveys but secretly sold it to commercial marketers.
CartManager International secretly collected consumer data through merchants' checkout pages and sold it to third-party marketers without disclosure.
Iconix collected personal data from roughly 1,000 children under 13 through fan and sweepstakes features without parental consent, violating COPPA and its own privacy policy.
RockYou failed to secure 32 million email addresses and passwords, and knowingly collected personal data from approximately 179,000 children without parental consent in violation of COPPA.
Artist Arena collected personal data from over 101,000 children under 13 for celebrity fan clubs without proper parental notice or consent.
Path's mobile app silently collected users' entire phone contact lists without consent and knowingly gathered personal data from thousands of children without parental approval.
C.A.L.M. Ventures used hidden monitoring software on rented computers to secretly spy on consumers in their homes, including activating webcams without consent.
Showplace secretly installed monitoring software on rented computers to capture consumers' webcam images, keystrokes, and personal data without their knowledge.
HTC introduced serious security vulnerabilities into millions of Android and Windows Mobile devices, exposing sensitive user data to third-party apps without permission.
Goldenshores Technologies' Brightest Flashlight Free app secretly transmitted users' precise geolocation and device identifiers to advertising networks without adequate disclosure.
PaymentsMD secretly used consumers' registration for a free billing portal to collect comprehensive health information from pharmacies and health plans for a separate fee-based service.
Craig Brittain operated a 'revenge porn' site, posting intimate photos of over 1,000 individuals without consent and running a sham removal service that charged victims to take down their own images.
InMobi secretly tracked users' locations without permission and collected personal data from children across thousands of apps without parental consent.

VIZIO covertly collected second-by-second television viewing data from millions of consumers by default and sold it to third parties while describing the feature only as providing 'program offers and suggestions.'
Lenovo preinstalled man-in-the-middle adware on consumer laptops that intercepted encrypted web traffic and created serious security vulnerabilities without adequate disclosure.
Explore Talent collected personal information from over 100,000 children without parental consent and used false promises of casting opportunities to sell paid memberships.

Sears secretly installed software on consumers' computers that tracked nearly all internet activity — including financial and health data from secure sessions — while describing it as simple 'online browsing' research.
BLU Products sold smartphones with preinstalled software that secretly transmitted users' text messages, location data, and contact lists to servers in China.
Musical.ly knowingly collected personal data from millions of children under 13 without parental notice or consent and failed to delete children's data when parents requested it.
UNIXIZ collected personal information from over 245,000 children on its gaming site without verifiable parental consent and with grossly inadequate data security.
Aleksandr Kogan and Alexander Nix built a Facebook app that falsely promised not to collect users' identifiable information while harvesting data from millions of users and their friends.
Zoom falsely claimed to offer end-to-end encryption for meetings and secretly installed software on Mac computers that bypassed Apple's security controls.

Ascension Data & Analytics handed mortgage documents containing sensitive consumer data to a vendor without conducting any security vetting, resulting in a cloud storage misconfiguration that exposed the data.
ITMedia collected consumers' sensitive loan application data under the pretext of connecting them to lenders, then sold it to marketers, debt negotiators, and unknown entities.
Kurbo by WW collected personal data from children under 13 without adequate parental notice or verifiable consent.
Microsoft collected personal information from children on Xbox Live before notifying parents or obtaining required parental consent, and retained incomplete-registration data for years.
Edmodo collected personal information from hundreds of thousands of children without parental consent and attempted to shift its COPPA compliance obligations onto schools.

X-Mode Social collected precise consumer location data through hundreds of apps and sold it—including sensitive locations like medical facilities—to government contractors without adequate disclosure or consumer consent.

Blackbaud's deficient security practices allowed a cyberattacker to remain undetected for months and exfiltrate millions of consumers' personal data, which the company then misrepresented in its breach notification.

Vivint's sales force fraudulently pulled third parties' credit reports without consent to qualify unqualified customers for financing, then passed those innocent parties' information to debt collectors.
Avast collected consumers' detailed browsing histories through its privacy-protection software and secretly sold that data to over 100 third parties without adequate disclosure or consent.
Apitor's robot toy app secretly collected precise geolocation data from child users via a third-party SDK without parental notice or consent.

patterns.name: Surveillance & Tracking
patterns.description: Undisclosed monitoring, location tracking, spyware, or stalkerware

cases.takeaway_brief: EchoMetrix sold parental monitoring software while secretly feeding children's online activity data to a third-party market research product sold to advertisers.
Chitika told consumers that clicking its opt-out button stopped behavioral advertising tracking, but the opt-out cookie expired after only 10 days without any notice.
Lookout Services falsely claimed 24/7 network security monitoring for its I-9 compliance product while lacking basic security safeguards like strong passwords and URL authentication controls.
ScanScout falsely told consumers they could opt out of tracking cookies by changing browser settings, when its Flash cookies were immune to browser-level controls.
Compete collected consumers' sensitive financial and personal information through tracking software while falsely claiming it only anonymously collected browsing data.
Aspen Way Enterprises installed hidden monitoring software on rented computers to secretly capture consumers' sensitive personal information, including via webcam.
B. Stamper Enterprises secretly monitored rented computer users via hidden software to capture passwords, medical records, and personal images.
C.A.L.M. Ventures used hidden monitoring software on rented computers to secretly spy on consumers in their homes, including activating webcams without consent.
DesignerWare developed and licensed stalkerware that secretly activated webcams, logged keystrokes, and tracked consumers' locations on rented computers.
J.A.G. Rents secretly monitored rented computer users through hidden software, capturing sensitive personal information and tricking consumers with fake registration pop-ups.
Red Zone Investment Group installed covert monitoring software on rented computers to secretly surveil users and collect personal information without their knowledge.
Showplace secretly installed monitoring software on rented computers to capture consumers' webcam images, keystrokes, and personal data without their knowledge.
Watershed Development secretly monitored rented computer users through hidden keylogging, screenshot, and webcam software without their knowledge or consent.
Aaron's provided its franchisees with spyware that secretly logged keystrokes, captured screenshots, and activated webcams on rented computers without consumers' knowledge or consent.
Goldenshores Technologies' Brightest Flashlight Free app secretly transmitted users' precise geolocation and device identifiers to advertising networks without adequate disclosure.
Snapchat falsely claimed messages disappeared permanently, that users received screenshot notifications, and that it did not collect location data, while also failing to secure user information.
Craig Brittain operated a 'revenge porn' site, posting intimate photos of over 1,000 individuals without consent and running a sham removal service that charged victims to take down their own images.
Turn Inc. falsely told consumers that deleting cookies would stop its tracking, while secretly using unkillable Verizon tracking headers to continue surveillance.
Lenovo preinstalled man-in-the-middle adware on consumer laptops that intercepted encrypted web traffic and created serious security vulnerabilities without adequate disclosure.

Sears secretly installed software on consumers' computers that tracked nearly all internet activity — including financial and health data from secure sessions — while describing it as simple 'online browsing' research.
BLU Products sold smartphones with preinstalled software that secretly transmitted users' text messages, location data, and contact lists to servers in China.
Retina-X sold covert device monitoring apps enabling stalking while falsely claiming consumers' data was kept private and secure.
MyEx.com publicly posted intimate images and personal information of individuals without their consent and charged victims thousands of dollars to have the content removed.
OpenX collected precise location data via a backdoor method that bypassed users' location permission denials, and collected children's personal data from child-directed apps without parental consent.
Credit Bureau Center used fake rental property ads to lure consumers into hidden paid credit monitoring subscriptions falsely advertised as free.
The Premom ovulation app secretly shared women's sensitive health and geolocation data with third parties for advertising despite explicit privacy promises.
Amazon retained children's Alexa voice recordings indefinitely and failed to honor user requests to delete voice and geolocation data despite explicit promises of full deletion control.
Rite Aid deployed inaccurate facial recognition technology without adequate safeguards, causing wrongful surveillance of innocent consumers including disproportionate harms to minority shoppers.

X-Mode Social collected precise consumer location data through hundreds of apps and sold it—including sensitive locations like medical facilities—to government contractors without adequate disclosure or consumer consent.
InMarket Media misled consumers about location data use in its apps and SDK, collecting precise location data for advertising profiling while telling consumers it was only for app functionality.
Verkada made false security claims for its building surveillance cameras, failed to implement basic security practices, and violated CAN-SPAM requirements in its marketing emails.
Gravy Analytics collected and sold precise mobile location data revealing consumers' sensitive characteristics — including health decisions and religious practices — without verifying user consent.

Mobilewalla collected and sold consumers' sensitive location data — including data revealing visits to medical facilities and places of worship — without meaningful consent and in violation of ad exchange terms.
Avast collected consumers' detailed browsing histories through its privacy-protection software and secretly sold that data to over 100 third parties without adequate disclosure or consent.
Apitor's robot toy app secretly collected precise geolocation data from child users via a third-party SDK without parental notice or consent.

patterns.name: Deceptive Product & Service Claims
patterns.description: False or misleading advertising about products, services, or business practices unrelated to data security or privacy

cases.takeaway_brief: EMC Mortgage made false representations to borrowers about loan balances and fees, charged unauthorized fees, and harassed borrowers in violation of multiple consumer protection laws.
Best Priced Brands deceived UK consumers by falsely presenting its U.S. businesses as UK-based retailers and misrepresenting prices, warranties, and consumer rights.
Spokeo marketed detailed consumer profiles for employment decisions while operating as an unregistered consumer reporting agency without any FCRA compliance procedures.
Filiquarian marketed mobile apps for employment background checks while operating as a consumer reporting agency without implementing any required FCRA procedures.
Instant Checkmate marketed background reports for employment screening purposes while failing to comply with any Fair Credit Reporting Act requirements.
Craig Brittain operated a 'revenge porn' site, posting intimate photos of over 1,000 individuals without consent and running a sham removal service that charged victims to take down their own images.
Jerk.com misrepresented that profile content was created by users and that paid memberships would provide meaningful dispute rights.
NoveltyExcuses.com sold fake financial documents—including pay stubs and insurance cards—designed to look authentic enough to deceive lenders and landlords.
Innovative Paycheck Solutions sold fake pay stubs and bank statements marketed as authentic-looking documents for use in deceiving lenders and landlords.
LifeLock falsely marketed its identity theft protection service as comprehensive and complete when it actually covered only a narrow subset of identity theft scenarios.
Office Depot used a fake diagnostic software tool that automatically reported false malware findings to sell unnecessary repair services to consumers.
BoostMyScore sold illegal credit piggybacking services and charged prohibited advance fees while falsely guaranteeing FICO score boosts.
Tapplock marketed its Internet-connected padlocks as 'unbreakable' and secure while critical physical and electronic vulnerabilities made them trivially easy to compromise.
Fashion Nova suppressed hundreds of thousands of negative customer reviews to create a falsely positive impression of its products.
IntelliVision marketed its facial recognition software as free of racial and gender bias and highly accurate when it had no testing to support those claims.

Aqua Finance funded home water treatment financing arrangements whose terms were systematically misrepresented by dealers and structured deceptively as open-end credit in violation of federal lending law.
Roca Labs falsely claimed its dietary supplement had a scientifically proven 90% weight-loss success rate and silenced unhappy customers with non-disparagement clauses.
Pornhub's operator actively distributed child sexual abuse material and non-consensual content for years while falsely claiming to promptly review and remove flagged material.

patterns.name: Algorithmic Harm
patterns.description: Biased or deceptive AI/automated decision-making, or algorithmic discrimination

cases.takeaway_brief: LAI Systems allowed third-party ad networks to collect persistent identifiers from children through its kids' apps for targeted advertising without parental notice or consent.
Henry Schein falsely marketed its dental software as providing industry-standard encryption for patient data when it actually used a weaker, proprietary algorithm.

Everalbum enabled facial recognition by default without user consent and used consumers' photos to train commercial AI without adequately disclosing this or deleting data when accounts were deactivated.
Rite Aid deployed inaccurate facial recognition technology without adequate safeguards, causing wrongful surveillance of innocent consumers including disproportionate harms to minority shoppers.
IntelliVision marketed its facial recognition software as free of racial and gender bias and highly accurate when it had no testing to support those claims.

patterns.name: Unfair Billing Practices
patterns.description: Unauthorized charges, deceptive pricing, hidden fees, or difficulty canceling

cases.takeaway_brief: Assail ran a telemarketing scam that swapped promised credit cards for worthless stored-value cards while making unauthorized debits from consumers' bank accounts.
Telemarketers falsely promised bad-credit consumers they were pre-approved for major credit cards, collected advance fees, and then never delivered the promised cards.
EMC Mortgage made false representations to borrowers about loan balances and fees, charged unauthorized fees, and harassed borrowers in violation of multiple consumer protection laws.
Overseas payday lenders offered loans without required disclosures and then threatened consumers with arrest and prosecution to coerce repayment, even on potentially unenforceable loans.
Time Warner Cable required consumers with weaker credit to pay deposits without providing the required risk-based pricing notices before they became contractually obligated.
Sprint charged consumers higher fees based on their credit reports but failed to provide required risk-based pricing notices before they became contractually obligated.
Craig Brittain operated a 'revenge porn' site, posting intimate photos of over 1,000 individuals without consent and running a sham removal service that charged victims to take down their own images.
Apartment Hunters charged fees for access to rental listings that were mostly inaccurate, unavailable, or identical to what was available for free online.
Office Depot used a fake diagnostic software tool that automatically reported false malware findings to sell unnecessary repair services to consumers.
BoostMyScore sold illegal credit piggybacking services and charged prohibited advance fees while falsely guaranteeing FICO score boosts.
MyEx.com publicly posted intimate images and personal information of individuals without their consent and charged victims thousands of dollars to have the content removed.
MyLife.com used deceptive teaser results suggesting searched individuals had criminal or sex offender records to sell subscriptions, and made cancellation deliberately difficult.

Cerebral secretly shared millions of patients' sensitive mental health and personal data with over twenty advertising platforms while falsely promising confidential, secure care and making it difficult to cancel subscriptions.

patterns.name: Dark Patterns & Deceptive Design
patterns.description: Trick interfaces, manipulative UX, hard-to-cancel subscriptions, or deceptive enrollment

cases.company_name: Paula L. Garrett, d/b/a Discreet Data Systems
cases.takeaway_brief: Paula Garrett ran an information brokerage that used impersonation and false pretenses to trick bank employees into disclosing customers' confidential account information, then sold that data.
Metropolitan Home Mortgage sent prescreened mortgage solicitations that lacked properly formatted opt-out notices as required by the FCRA and the Prescreen Rule.
Chitika told consumers that clicking its opt-out button stopped behavioral advertising tracking, but the opt-out cookie expired after only 10 days without any notice.
Google auto-enrolled Gmail users into its Buzz social network using their contacts, breaking promises that Gmail data would only be used for email.
ScanScout falsely told consumers they could opt out of tracking cookies by changing browser settings, when its Flash cookies were immune to browser-level controls.
J.A.G. Rents secretly monitored rented computer users through hidden software, capturing sensitive personal information and tricking consumers with fake registration pop-ups.
Nomi Technologies promised consumers opt-out rights at retail locations while never actually providing any in-store opt-out mechanism.
TaxSlayer, a tax preparation service handling highly sensitive financial data, lacked a written security program, performed no risk assessments, and buried its privacy notice in a license agreement.
Epic Games used dark patterns to charge consumers — including children — for Fortnite purchases without informed consent, and denied account access to those who disputed charges.

patterns.name: Identity Theft Facilitation
patterns.description: Enabling or failing to prevent identity theft, or fraudulently obtaining personal information

cases.company_name: Paula L. Garrett, d/b/a Discreet Data Systems
cases.takeaway_brief: Paula Garrett ran an information brokerage that used impersonation and false pretenses to trick bank employees into disclosing customers' confidential account information, then sold that data.
ISIS advertised and sold confidential consumer phone records and financial account information obtained by impersonating account holders without their authorization.
Information Search, Inc. obtained consumers' confidential bank account data by impersonating customers to financial institution employees and then sold that information to clients.
CEO Group sold confidential consumer telephone call records obtained through impersonation and false pretenses without account holders' knowledge or authorization.
Action Research Group impersonated account holders to fraudulently obtain confidential telephone records from carriers and sold them to third-party clients.
Accusearch obtained consumers' confidential phone records by impersonating account holders and then sold those records to paying clients without consumers' knowledge.
ChoicePoint failed to verify the identities of prospective data subscribers, allowing fraudulent actors to access the personal information of approximately 163,000 consumers.
LifeLock falsely marketed its identity theft protection service as comprehensive and complete when it actually covered only a narrow subset of identity theft scenarios.
Alliance Security and its CEO made over two million illegal telemarketing calls including to Do Not Call registrants, impersonated ADT, and obtained consumer reports without permissible purpose.
Kohl's denied identity theft victims access to transaction records about fraudulent purchases made in their names.

patterns.name: Illegal Telemarketing
patterns.description: Do Not Call violations, robocalls, TSR violations, and deceptive telemarketing

cases.takeaway_brief: Assail ran a telemarketing scam that swapped promised credit cards for worthless stored-value cards while making unauthorized debits from consumers' bank accounts.
Alliance Security and its CEO made over two million illegal telemarketing calls including to Do Not Call registrants, impersonated ADT, and obtained consumer reports without permissible purpose.
